coredata.dc:title: A novel label-free photoelectrochemical sensor based on N,S-GQDs and CdS co-sensitized hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube for detection of cardiac troponin I
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Dawei
Bao, Chunzhu
Khan, Malik Saddam
Wang, Chuanlei
Zhang, Yong
Liu, Qinze
Zhang, Xian
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A novel label-free photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor based on graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N,S-GQDs) and CdS co-sensitized hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube was fabricated to detect cardiac troponin I (cTnI). The unique hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube was synthesized successfully by the solvothermal method, which has a large specific surface to load functional materials. N,S-GQDs nanoparticles were assembled to the surface of cubic Zn2SnO4 coated ITO electrode, which efficiently accelerated the electronic transition and improved photo-to-current conversion efficiency. Then, CdS nanoparticles further were modified by in-situ growth method to form Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite with prominent photocurrent, which was 30 times that of the Zn2SnO4 cube alone. In this work, the specific immune recognition between cTnI antigens and cTnI antibodies (anti-cTnI) reduced the intensity of the photoelectric signal. And the intensity decreased linearly with the logarithm of cTnI concentration range from 0.001 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL with a detection limit of 0.3 pg/mL. With high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, good stability and reproducibility, the fabricated PEC sensor showed promising applications in the sensor, clinical diagnosis of myocardial infarction and PEC analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical sensor
Zn2SnO4
N,S-GQDs
CdS
Cardiac troponin I

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the fabricated novel PEC sensor
Application of the fabricated PEC sensor in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project of China (No. 21627809 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775053 , 21575050 and 51403111 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2017MB027 ). Dawei Fan thanks the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2016M592122 ), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( 201603060 ), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of University of Jinan ( XBH1517 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The construction process of photoelectrochemical sensors.

SEM images of PPy/g-C3N4-VN (A), Zn2SnO4 prepared without template of PPy/g-C3N4-VN (B), cubic Zn2SnO4 particles (C), magnified cubic Zn2SnO4 particles (D), Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs (E), Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite (F). EDS images of cubic Zn2SnO4 particles (G), Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs (H) and Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite (I).

TEM images of Zn2SnO4 cube (A) and N,S-GQDs (B). Diameter distribution histograms (inset in B) of N,S-GQDs. XRD patterns obtained from cubic Zn2SnO4 (C). XRD images of N,S-GQDs (D). FT-IR spectrum of N,S-GQDs (E). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Zn2SnO4 (F). UV–vis spectra of N,S-GQDs (G). The inset in G was magnified UV–vis spectra of N,S-GQDs. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Zn2SnO4, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS (H).

(A) and (B) Time-based photocurrent response curves and (C) EIS Nyquist plots of (a) ITO, (b) ITO/Zn2SnO4, (c) ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs, (d) Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS, (e) Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS), (f) Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/anti-cTnI, (g) Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/anti-cTnI/BSA, (h) Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/anti-cTnI/BSA/cTnI. The inset in C was Randles equivalent circuit for EIS. The applied potential was 0 V. (D) The schematic of electron transfer of Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS PEC sensor in AA electrolyte.

(A) Photocurrent response and (B) the logarithmic calibration curve for PEC sensor to detect cTnI with different concentrations. (C) Photocurrent response of the PEC sensor under 15 on/off illumination cycles for 320 s, c cTnI = 10 ng/mL. (D) Selectivity of the PEC sensor to detect cTnI. (a) Blank, (b) Blank + 100 ng/mL CEA, (c) Blank + 100 ng/mL SCCA, (d) Blank + 100 ng/mL PSA, (e) 1 ng/mL cTnI, (f) 1 ng/mL cTnI + 100 ng/mL CEA, (g) 1 ng/mL cTnI + 100 ng/mL SCCA, (h) 1 ng/mL cTnI +100 ng/mL PSA. The applied potential was 0 V.
Determination of cTnI added in human serum with the fabricated PEC sensor.

head.ce:title: A novel label-free photoelectrochemical sensor based on N,S-GQDs and CdS co-sensitized hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube for detection of cardiac troponin I

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel label-free photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor based on graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N,S-GQDs) and CdS co-sensitized hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube was fabricated to detect cardiac troponin I (cTnI). The unique hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube was synthesized successfully by the solvothermal method, which has a large specific surface to load functional materials. N,S-GQDs nanoparticles were assembled to the surface of cubic Zn2SnO4 coated ITO electrode, which efficiently accelerated the electronic transition and improved photo-to-current conversion efficiency. Then, CdS nanoparticles further were modified by in-situ growth method to form Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite with prominent photocurrent, which was 30 times that of the Zn2SnO4 cube alone. In this work, the specific immune recognition between cTnI antigens and cTnI antibodies (anti-cTnI) reduced the intensity of the photoelectric signal. And the intensity decreased linearly with the logarithm of cTnI concentration range from 0.001 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL with a detection limit of 0.3 pg/mL. With high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, good stability and reproducibility, the fabricated PEC sensor showed promising applications in the sensor, clinical diagnosis of myocardial infarction and PEC analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis, a dynamically promising technique, has gained more and more attention (Fan et al., 2017a, 2017b; Liu et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2009b; Yang et al., 2004). PEC sensor has become one of the most potential detection tools in the fields of life sciences, environmental science, agricultural science and food safety detection (Fan et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2007; Xia et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2006), owe to its distinct advantages, such as rapid test, desirable sensitivity, low background signal, low cost, simple devices and so on (Freeman et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2009a; Zhu et al., 2000).

As an important semiconductor material with excellent properties, zinc stannate (Zn2SnO4), has been extensively applied in solar cells, gas sensors, photocatalysts and lithium ions battery (Lin et al., 2009; Rong et al., 2006; Tan et al., 2007). Currently, various Zn2SnO4 nanostructures have been synthesized such as irregular particle morphology, nanorods, nanowires and well-defined polyhedra (Bai and Zhou, 2014; Lana-Villarreal et al., 2007; Zhao et al., 2007). The cubic Zn2SnO4 has unique properties, including high specific surface area, low density and better penetration (Yang et al., 2010). However, Zn2SnO4 was difficult to be efficiently excited in visible-light due to its wide band-gap (Zhao et al., 2016). Therefore, it was necessary to enhance the visible-light activity of Zn2SnO4 particles. In this study, a new kind of hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube was prepared successfully, then N,S-GQDs and CdS with narrow-band gaps were employed to co-sensitize Zn2SnO4, which accelerated the electronic transition and enhanced the photoelectric properties of Zn2SnO4.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was a sort of single or few-layer graphenes and have satisfying conductivity, adjustable band gap and unique photoelectric properties (Xia et al., 2017). Thus, they have been used in many fields such as photocatalysis (Roushani et al., 2015), ion detection (Cai et al., 2014), biological imaging (Tan et al., 2015) and electrochemical biosensor (Zhang et al., 2015). The major disadvantage of GQDs based PEC sensor was that the sensitivity and selectivity were limited due to the nonspecificity of GQDs. However, many researches recently have shown that the photoelectric properties of GQDs could be effectively regulated by doping heteroatoms (Cai et al., 2017a, 2017b; Kundu et al., 2017). In particular, doping N and S could effectively modulate their band gap and electronic density to enhance the absorption of visible light. Hence, the graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N,S-GQDs) were devoted to sensitize Zn2SnO4, which could efficiently accelerate the electronic transition and improve photo-to-current conversion efficiency. Meanwhile, as an excellent nanomaterial, CdS has narrow band-gap (2.4 eV) and excellent absorption to visible light (Liu et al., 2016). Therefore, CdS nanoparticles were assembled by in-situ growth method to form Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite, which could further enhance the photoelectric signal in visible light.

In our work, a novel label-free PEC sensor based on N,S-GQDs and CdS co-sensitized hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cube was constructed to detect cardiac troponin I (cTnI). Throughout the ages, heart attacks and cardiovascular diseases have been harmful to the health of human. As one of the subunits of cardiac troponin complexes, cTnI was biochemical markers of myocardial damage (Zhou et al., 2014). At present, cTnI has been increasingly applied in the diagnosis of diseases such as myocardial infarction and stenocardia, owing to its myocardial specificity and excellent sensitivity (Guo et al., 2005; Jo et al., 2017). Hence, cTnI was employed as the testing target to appraise the label-free photoelectrochemical sensor.

The cTnI and cTnI antibodies (anti-cTnI) were supplied by Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd. ZnCl2 was obtained from Tianjin Commie Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. SnCl4·5H2O and pyrrole were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Thiourea and Cd(NO3)2 were obtained from Guoyao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Na2S and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd, China. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q, Millipore) used in all experiments was deionized to 18.25 MΩ cm. The other materials were provided in Electronic Supporting information (ESI†).

The PEC tests were obtained using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation, which was purchased from Chenhua Instrument Shanghai Co., Ltd, China. Meanwhile, a 100 W LED lamp of white light was used for an irradiation source. The wavelength range of LED lamp (Fig. S1) and other details were shown in ESI†.

The preparation of Zn2SnO4 cubes were as follows. Firstly, a certain amount of polypyrrole/N-vacancy graphitic carbon nitride (PPy/g-C3N4-VN) (the synthesis was shown in EIS†) were added into a mixed solution containing 20 mL ultrapure water and ethanol (1:1) with ultrasound for 0.5 h. Secondly, 1.25 mmol ZnCl2 and 0.625 mmol SnCl4·5H2O were dissolved in the above solution. Then, the NaOH aqueous solution (5.0 mL, 1.0 mol/L) was added. The obtained solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) after stirring for 15 min, and then heated at 200 °C for 24 h. Separated using centrifugation and then cleaned three times with ultrapure water, the precipitates were acquired after drying.

The N, S-GQDs were synthesized according to previous report (Qu et al., 2013). 2.5 mmol of citric acid and 10.0 mmol of thiourea were added into ultrapure water (12 mL). The above solution was poured into autoclave (50 mL) after stirring for 5 min, and then heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After adding a certain amount of ethanol, the precipitates were separated from the solution with centrifugation (9000 rpm) and then washed three times.

Before assembled of sensor, ITO slices were cut into 2.5 × 0.8 cm2 pieces with ultrasonic for 30 min in acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water, separately. Under the nitrogen stream, the ITO substrates were dried for the preparation of sensor. Construction procedure of the novel PEC sensor was demonstrated in Fig. 1. Firstly, 4 mg/mL of Zn2SnO4 aqueous solution was disposed with ultrasonic for 0.5 h. Then, the 10 μL of Zn2SnO4 suspension was coated onto a ITO electrode. After drying in air, 1 mg/mL N,S-GQDs solution (4 μL) was dropped on the above electrode, followed by drying in air for 0.5 h. After rinsing, 4 μL of Cd(NO3)2 and Na2S solution were dropped successively on the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs electrodes for 0.5 h by in-situ growth method and then washed with ultrapure water. Subsequently, 4 μL of 0.1 mol/L TGA was dropped onto the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS electrode for 0.5 h and then washed with ultrapure water. Then, 4 μL of EDC/NHS (0.01 mol/L / 0.002 mol/L) solution was dropped on the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA electrode for 0.5 h to activate -COOH of TGA. After removing the redundant EDC/NHS by washing, 5 μL anti-cTnI was dropped onto the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS) electrode to incubate for 0.5 h and then rinsed to remove the loosely bounded anti-cTnI. After that, 5 μL of 1 mg/mL BSA was dropped onto the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/anti-cTnI electrode to block nonspecific sites for 40 min. After rinsed with ultrapure water, different concentrations of cTnI solution (5 μL) was added to the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/anti-cTnI/BSA electrode severally and then incubated for 0.5 h. Ultimately, the fabricated PEC sensor was washed with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C.

The SEM images of PPy/g-C3N4-VN, Zn2SnO4 without PPy/g-C3N4-VN as template (EIS†) and cube-like hierarchical Zn2SnO4 were showed in Fig. 2A, B and C, respectively. It was found that the Zn2SnO4 structure synthesized without PPy/g-C3N4-VN was irregular and varies in size. However, in the presence of PPy/g-C3N4-VN, the unique structural Zn2SnO4 was obtained. The magnified SEM image of Zn2SnO4 (Fig. 2D) demonstrated that Zn2SnO4 was a hierarchical cubic structure, including nanostructures, based on PPy/g-C3N4-VN templates (the function of PPy/g-C3N4-VN was analyzed in ESI†). EDS image (Fig. 2G) of cube-like hierarchical Zn2SnO4 showed that the Zn2SnO4 cube contained only Zn, Sn and O elements. It was relatively pure and free of other impurities. In addition, the hierarchical structure of Zn2SnO4 cube became indistinct after modified by N,S-GQDs from the SEM of Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs (Fig. 2E). Elements of carbon and sulfur were assigned to N,S-GQDs in EDS image (Fig. 2H) of Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs, which distinctly testified that N,S-GQDs were modified on the surface of Zn2SnO4 successfully. After that, the SEM images of Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS (Fig. 2F) clearly indicated there were many spherical CdS particles in the Zn2SnO4 particles. Meanwhile, the carboxyl group of N,S-GQDs combined with Zn2SnO4 through the build of Zn–O bond (Jiang et al., 2012). Then, Cd2+ ions were adsorbed on the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs electrode through electrostatic interaction between Cd2+ from Cd(NO3)2 and -COO- of N,S-GQDs. CdS nanoparticles formed on the surface of Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs modified electrode by in-situ growth method. The EDS image (Fig. 2I) further proved the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite was successfully prepared. As a comparison experiment, Cd(NO3)2 and Na2S were directly modified on the ITO/Zn2SnO4 electrode to obtain supposed ITO/Zn2SnO4/CdS electrode. The EDS image of supposed Zn2SnO4/CdS has not Cd element (Fig. S2I), which manifested CdS could not combine with Zn2SnO4 without the assistance of N,S-GODs. Therefore, nanoscale N,S-GQDs were favorable to the in-situ growth of CdS nanoparticles on Zn2SnO4 cubes.

The TEM image of Zn2SnO4 cube showed it was solid in Fig. 3A, and the band-gap of cubic Zn2SnO4 was measured as 3.83 eV (Fig. 3F). In Fig. 3B, the diameter of N,S-GQDs was around 3.8 nm, its size was favorable to combine with Zn2SnO4. In addition, the typical XRD profiles for the cubic Zn2SnO4 and the XRD image of N,S-GQDs were exhibited in Fig. 3C, D. It was obvious that all of the observed diffraction peaks in Fig. 3C could be indexed to the standard cubic Zn2SnO4 (JCPDF No. 24-1470). No diffraction peaks from other phases or possible impurities were detected. Meanwhile, the N,S-GQDs showed a broader diffraction peak at 23.2° in Fig. 3D, corresponding to the previous report (Gavgani et al., 2016). This result indicated that the cubic Zn2SnO4 and N,S-GQDs were prepared successfully.

The FT-IR spectrum of N,S-GQDs (Fig. 3E) showed the wide absorption band at 3000–3500 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H. This demonstrated that there were more amino and hydroxyl groups in the N,S-GQDs, due to the highly hydrophilic property of N,S-GQDs (Gavgani et al., 2016). The bands at 1707 cm−1 and 1665 cm−1 belonged to the vibrational absorption band of C˭O in COOH and CONH, respectively. While the peaks at 1577 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibrations of C˭C and C-N, respectively. Moreover, the bands at 1089, and 633 cm−1 attributes to the stretching C˭S and C-S, respectively. This further proved that N,S-GQDs was synthesized well. Combining the UV–vis spectra of N,S-GQDs (Fig. 3G) with the photos of the N,S-GQD aqueous solution under natural light and a UV light of 365 nm (Fig. S3), it was obvious that the N,S-GQDs owned certain absorption capability in visible light. Meanwhile, Fig. 3H depicted the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Zn2SnO4, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS. Among them, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite showed a broad and excellent absorption to visible light, demonstrating that the co-sensitization of N,S-GQDs and CdS improved the absorption to visible light of composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fabricated novel PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A and B illustrated the photocurrent-time curves of modified ITO electrodes in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) containing AA (0.1 mol/L). The photocurrent of bare ITO (curve a) electrode was close to 0 µA. After modified Zn2SnO4 on ITO electrode, the photocurrent of ITO/Zn2SnO4 (curve b) increased. Meanwhile, the photocurrents of ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs electrode (curve c) doubly increased compared with that of ITO/Zn2SnO4. The signal suddenly increased to 30 µA, while CdS was combined with ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs electrode by in-situ growth method (curve d). Combined with the above of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Zn2SnO4, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs, Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS (Fig. 3H), it could be inferred that N, S-GQDs and CdS could promote the formation of electron hole pairs, making the composites have high visible light activity. Therefore, the ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS electrodes would be used to construct the following PEC sensor. Subsequently, in order to prove the interaction among of Zn2SnO4, N,S-GOD and CdS, the time-based photocurrent response curves of ITO/CdS and ITO/N,S-GQDs/CdS were further investigated, the photocurrents of which were 1.2 and 7.5 µA, respectively (Fig. S4). Compared with the photocurrents of ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs (0.165 µA) and ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS (30 µA) from Fig. 4A, B, it was obvious that the photocurrent intensity of ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS achieved maximum. The result effectively indicated that the synergistic effect of Zn2SnO4, N,S-GQDs and CdS with each other promoted to gain Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite with excellent visible-light activity. When TGA was added to the base material and EDC/NHS was used to activate the carboxyl group of the TGA, the photocurrent signal was reduced to 26 µA (curve e). The amidation of EDC/NHS was utilized to bind anti-cTnI onto the ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS) electrode. As a kind of organics, EDC/NHS could impede the photoelectron transfer due to the decrease of photocurrent signal. After that, as the anti-cTnI (curve f), BSA (curve g) and cTnI (curve h) were combined successively on the ITO electrode surface, the photocurrent intensity decreased gradually. The photocurrent signals were reduced on account of protein molecules and insulating molecules of anti-cTnI, BSA, cTnI, which could hamper electron transfer, leading to the recombination of photo-generated electrons and electron-holes.

To further prove the successful establishment of the PEC sensor, EIS of assembly electrodes were investigated in a 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.10 mol/L KCl. EIS was an efficient means to describe the electrode transfer resistance (R et), which could change with the embellishment of the electrode (Gao et al., 2015). The semicircle diameter of the EIS Nyquist plots reflected the R et value (Gao et al., 2015). The inset in Fig. 4C was Randles equivalent circuit simulated with ZSimWin software, whose values were in Table S1 (ESI†). Moreover, Fig. 4C revealed the EIS Nyquist plots of assembled electrodes in each step. It was obvious that the semicircle diameter of the blank electrode (curve a) corresponded to the smaller R et value. With the ornament of Zn2SnO4 and N,S-GQDs, the semicircle diameter gradually became larger (curve b, c), implying that Zn2SnO4 and N,S-GQDs successfully combined with ITO electrode. After modified with CdS particles formed by in-situ growth method, the semicircle diameter was significantly increased. CdS was a semiconductor material, which hindered electron conduction after forming on the surface of ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs electrode. On this basis, the electrode was modified with TGA and EDC/NHS. Among them, TGA, as the connectome between -COOH of the CdS nanoparticles and -NH2 of the anti-cTnI, has resulted in further increasing of R et values, due to its insulating properties. The EDC/NHS activated -COOH on the TGA and facilitated the better combination between carboxyl functionalized TGA and anti-cTnI. And as a protein, its insulating properties also increased the semicircular diameter of the electrode. Meanwhile, comparing with the above decorated materials, the hindrance was more apparent and the R et value would increase significantly when the electrode surface modified with macromolecular protein substance such as anti-cTnI, BSA and cTnI. In conclusion, the EIS Nyquist plots proved that the PEC sensor assembly was successful.

The electron transfer mechanism of Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS PEC sensor was proposed in PBS electrolyte containing AA (Fig. 4D). Under the visible light, the low photo-generated electrons and holes generated in Zn2SnO4 on account of its broad band-gap of 3.83 eV (Fig. 3F), resulting in the low PEC activity. However, the N,S-GQDs and CdS were semiconductor materials with narrow band gaps, which promoted the separation of photo-generated electrons and holes (Tian et al., 2017). Herein, the Zn2SnO4 was sensitized by N,S-GQDs and CdS to acquire Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composites, which could possess the excellent absorption to the visible light. When Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite were stimulated by visible light, the photo-generated electrons transferred from the valence band (VB) of CdS to the conduction band (CB). Due to the interfacial adhesion between CdS and N,S-GQDs, the photo-generated electrons could transfer to the CB of N,S-GQDs, then they could transfer to the CB of Zn2SnO4. Meanwhile, AA was a distinguished electron donor, which hampered the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes. Thus, it facilitated electrons transfer from CB to ITO and led to the augment of photocurrent signals. It was notable that the CB and VB edges of Zn2SnO4, N,S-GQDs and CdS were gradually increased in favor of the transfer of photo-generated carriers. Consequently, PEC sensor with excellent performance in visible-light irradiation was constructed successfully.

It could be seen from Fig. S5 (ESI†), the experiment mainly studied the influence of the concentration of Cd(NO3)2, Na2S, AA and pH on the photocurrent signals of the ITO/Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS electrode.

In order to assure the complete reaction of Cd2+, the concentration of Na2S was fixed as 0.1 mol/L and adequate comparing with Cd(NO3)2. Fig. S5A demonstrated that the photoelectric signal has been increasing within the concentration of Cd(NO3)2 from 0.02 to 0.08 mol/L. Further, when the concentration of Cd(NO3)2 was higher than 0.08 mol/L, the electrical signal appeared on the platform. Therefore, the concentration of Cd(NO3)2 of 0.08 mol/L was optimal. In order to further study the effect of varied CdS quantity to photoelectric signal, the multiple of optimal concentration ratio of Cd(NO3)2 and Na2S (0.08 mol/L:0.1 mol/L) were investigated at 0.5, 1, 2, 5 times (i.e. the concentrations of Cd(NO3)2 and Na2S were 0.04:0.05, 0.08:0.1, 0.16:0.2, 0.4:0.5, respectively.) (Fig. S5B). It was obvious that the photoelectric signal achieved optimum when the concentrations of Cd(NO3)2 and Na2S were 0.08 mol/L and 0.1 mol/L, respectively. This phenomenon could be explained that a small quantity of CdS nanoparticles formed was insufficient leading to the decrease of photocurrent intensity. Meanwhile, the excess CdS nanoparticles weaken the synergistic effect of Zn2SnO4, N,S-GQDs and CdS, resulted in poor photoelectric signal. In the same way, Fig. S5C indicated that the photoelectric signals increased with pH in the range from 5 to 7.4. However, when the pH value was higher than 7.4, the electrical signal appeared on the platform. The pH is 7.4, which was similar with the physiological environment and fit for the activity of the immobilized protein. Therefore, the photoelectronic signal achieved highest when pH is 7.4. Hence, 7.4 was the optimal pH of the solution. As shown in Fig. S5D, in the range from 0 to 0.1 mol/L, the photoelectric signal increased to about 30 µA with the increase of AA concentration. When the concentration augmented from 0.1 mol/L to 1.0 mol/L, the photoelectric signal only increased 3 µA. Moreover, as the concentration of AA further increased, the photoelectric signal gradually decreased. The photocurrent signal reached a maximum value at 0.1 mol/L AA, which could be ascribed to the saturation of electron donor when AA concentration higher than 0.1 mol/L. Thus, 0.1 moL/L was selected as the optimal concentration of AA.

The novel PEC electrodes modified with different concentrations of cTnI was carried out in the optimal conditions. From the photocurrent curves ( Fig. 5A), as the concentrations of cTnI increased, the photocurrent reduced gradually because of the insulation properties of cTnI, which indicated the prepared PEC sensor was appropriate for cTnI detection. In addition, the photocurrent reduced linearly with the logarithm of cTnI concentration range from 0.001 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL (Fig. 5B). The linear equation was I = 13.48–2.587 log (c cTnI, ng/mL) with a correlation coefficient of 0.997. The detection limit was 0.3 pg/mL calculated based on the literature (Ren et al., 2015).

Fig. 5C demonstrated the photocurrent response of the sensor modified with 10 ng/mL cTnI under 15 times on/off illumination cycles for 320 s. Over time, there was no obvious variation in the photoelectric current, indicating the good stability of the prepared photoelectrochemical sensor. The reproducibility of the PEC sensor was evaluated by the measurement of 10 ng/mL cTnI. The photocurrents of the five examined ITO electrodes were 10.91 μA, 11.05 μA, 11.33 μA, 10.55 μA and 10.95 μA, severally. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.6%, turning out that the reproducibility and precision of the fabricated PEC sensor were acceptable.

Fig. 5D showed the selectivity of the PEC sensor for cTnI detection, which validated its practicability. The photocurrent signals were researched by blending 1 ng/mL of cTnI with 100 ng/mL of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), 100 ng/mL squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) and 100 ng/mL prostate specific antigen (PSA). After 100 ng/mL of CEA, SCCA or PSA was incubated on the blank electrode (Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/anti-cTnI/BSA) either mixed with cTnI or not, there was no evident change of photocurrent signal. The results illustrated the fabricated PEC sensor possessed excellent specificity and selectivity for cTnI detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the fabricated PEC sensor in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: By standard addition methods, the recoveries of cTnI with different concentrations (18.0, 22.0 and 28.0 pg/mL) in human serum were confirmed, which further prove the practicability of the fabricated PEC sensor. In Table 1, the detected cTnI concentration of human serum was 22.7 pg/mL, the RSD was in the range of 3.17–4.14%, and the recoveries of human serum were in the range from 96.2% to 106%. To further validate the novel PEC sensor, the comparison of different methods for the detection of cTnI was shown in Table S2 (ESI†). The satisfactory results revealed the promising application of the fabricated PEC sensor in blood sample detection.

In brief, a novel label-free PEC sensor for supersensitive detection of cTnI has been constructed successfully. With a template of PPy/g-C3N4-VN, the unique hierarchical Zn2SnO4 cubes were synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis method, which has a large specific surface to load functional materials. Afterwards, N,S-GQDs were applied to sensitize Zn2SnO4 cube through the build of Zn-O bond aim to accelerate the electronic transition and improve photo-to-current conversion efficiency. Then, CdS nanoparticles were modified on Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs composite by the in-situ growth method to obtain the Zn2SnO4/N,S-GQDs/CdS composite. The composite exhibited admirable visible-light absorption and excellent PEC response in the presence of ascorbic acid, which was 30 times that of the Zn2SnO4 cube alone. Moreover, the proposed PEC sensor exhibited a broad detection range from 0.001 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL and a low detection limit of 0.3 pg/mL. With high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, good stability and reproducibility, the supersensitive PEC sensor revealed hopeful actual applications in analytical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project of China (No. 21627809), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775053, 21575050 and 51403111), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2017MB027). Dawei Fan thanks the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M592122), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Shandong Province (201603060), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of University of Jinan (XBH1517).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.01.050.
